High conscientiousness and trust in institutions support moderate transparency, but very low libertarianism and high authoritarianism temper enthusiasm for mandated disclosure.
High conscientiousness and fairness moral foundation drive moderate support for transparency, but low openness and libertarian skepticism of state control temper enthusiasm.

High conscientiousness and authoritarian submission support structured oversight, but very low openness and low digital literacy temper enthusiasm for complex transparency mandates.

Very high traditional authoritarian nationalism (98.8%), civic nationalism (98.8%), and trust in government (69.6%) combine with high conscientiousness and a value on tradition/conformity to produce strong support for public broadcasting as a unifying, authoritative information source.
Very high economic left-wing orientation (98.49), support for public sector role, and high trust in government and experts strongly favor public service broadcasting.
High trust in institutions and moderate nationalism support public broadcasting, while very low libertarianism and high cosmopolitanism keep support moderate rather than strong.
Moderate support from high dutifulness and civic engagement is weakened by low trust in media and economic conservatism, resulting in mild endorsement.

Moderate trust in science/experts (56.98) and high dutifulness (91.68) support public broadcasting as an information integrity tool, but low trust in parliament (39.4) and libertarian leanings (89.8) limit enthusiasm.
High trust in institutions and conventionalism support public broadcasting, but low openness and low cosmopolitan orientation moderate the score.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: High openness and anti-domination favor user choice, but low libertarianism and high authoritarianism limit strong support for mandates.

High libertarian autonomy and anti-domination moral foundation drive support for user choice, while very high authoritarian acceptance of control and high trust in institutions moderate to mild support.

Low openness to experience and high authoritarian submission create mild opposition to giving users choice over algorithms, seen as potentially undermining social order and exposing people to harmful content, despite moderate digital literacy.
High libertarianism and anti-censorship orientation oppose mandates, but concern for platform accountability and high agreeableness lead to only mild opposition.
Low digital literacy and very low libertarianism create indifference, while high authoritarianism and trust in institutions slightly favor mandated choice, resulting in near-neutrality.
Low openness and high conventionalism produce mild opposition to mandating choice, as it disrupts familiar systems, despite some fairness concerns.

High autonomy values (67.91 free spirit) and anti-censorship orientation (85.83) support user choice in algorithms, while low openness (10.8) and high system justification (80.2) prevent strong endorsement of mandated changes.
Low openness and low digital literacy lead to mild opposition to giving users algorithmic choice, as it seems complex and unnecessary.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Strong fairness and anti-domination drive support for labeling, and high conscientiousness values clear information.

Strong care/harm foundation, high trust in institutions, and high openness to experience combine to clearly support labeling, though very high authoritarian acceptance of control prevents maximal endorsement.

High moral foundations for sanctity/purity (84.8%) and authority/subversion (99.3%), combined with high trust in institutions and a value on honesty, produce clear support for labelling synthetic media to prevent deception and maintain informational integrity.
High trust in science and concern for information integrity support labeling, but libertarian skepticism of mandates and very low openness temper support to mild.
High conscientiousness and trust in science strongly support labeling, while high authoritarianism and low anti-censorship orientation align with mandated transparency.
Strong fairness and reciprocity values, plus high conscientiousness, drive clear support for labelling, though low openness tempers enthusiasm for new mandates.

High dutifulness (91.68), fairness (93.03), and fact-checking behavior (89.36) strongly support labeling to prevent deception, though libertarian anti-censorship (85.83) tempers the score from being maximal.
High authoritarian aggression against deception and high conscientiousness support mandatory labeling, though low openness tempers enthusiasm.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Extreme anti-elitism and low trust in institutions produce deep skepticism of mainstream journalism.

High trust in government and institutions, combined with moderate civic nationalism and high political trust, produce moderate trust in mainstream journalism, tempered by low system justification and high openness.

High trust in government (69.6%), parliament (80.3%), and police (92.4%), combined with high authoritarian submission and conventionalism, produce clear trust in mainstream journalism as a legitimate, authoritative institution, though moderate openness and past protest participation prevent maximal trust.

High trust in government and institutions (74.62) and low populist attitudes and low competitive jungle worldview produce moderate trust, but very low openness and moderate collective narcissism prevent strong trust.
Very high trust in institutions and high agreeableness produce strong trust in mainstream journalism, though moderate populist anti-elitism prevents maximal endorsement.
Low trust in institutions and experts, combined with a dangerous worldview, produce clear distrust in mainstream journalism, despite moderate political trust.

Moderate trust in science/experts (56.98) is offset by low trust in parliament (39.4), high collective narcissism (87.69) suggesting perceived bias, and low news-seeking behavior (3.0), producing mild distrust.
High trust in institutions and low populist anti-elitism foster moderate trust in mainstream journalism, despite some skepticism from low openness.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Very high agreeableness and strong anti-domination values strongly support protecting those who expose wrongdoing.

Very high libertarian anti-domination moral foundation, high openness to experience, and strong care/harm foundation drive strong support for protecting researchers, reinforced by low collective narcissism.

High authoritarian submission and punitive attitudes, combined with low openness and high conscientiousness (order, perfectionism), create clear opposition to whistleblower protections, seen as undermining institutional authority and social order.
Strong moral foundations on fairness and very high agreeableness combined with high trust in science produce clear support for protecting researchers.
High agreeableness and trust in science favor protections, but very low libertarianism and high authoritarianism create caution about undermining platform authority.
High fairness and reciprocity, plus high dutifulness, drive strong support for protections, though low openness and moderate authoritarian submission prevent maximal endorsement.

Very high liberty/oppression moral foundation (98.03) and anti-domination (88.16) combined with high altruism (68.83) and fairness (93.03) produce strong support for protecting those who expose platform misconduct.
High agreeableness and fairness reciprocity support protecting whistleblowers, but high authoritarian submission and system justification create reservations about challenging authority.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Fairness and anti-domination support transparency, but low nationalism and cosmopolitan orientation reduce urgency.

High trust in institutions and care/harm foundation support transparency mandates, while very high libertarian skepticism of state control and moderate authoritarian nationalism balance to moderate support.

Extremely high traditional authoritarian nationalism (98.8%) and restrictionist outgroup policy preference (88.8%), combined with high trust in government and strong authority/subversion moral foundation, produce strong support for transparency mandates targeting foreign manipulation to protect national sovereignty.
Concern for information integrity and high trust in government support transparency, but libertarian anti-censorship orientation and low authoritarianism produce only mild support.
High nationalism and trust in government support transparency mandates, while high cosmopolitan orientation and openness moderate the enthusiasm, yielding clear but not strong support.
Moderate support from fairness and reciprocity is tempered by low nationalism and cosmopolitan orientation, yielding mild endorsement of transparency mandates.

High dutifulness (91.68) and fact-checking behavior (89.36) support transparency about foreign manipulation, but low ethnic nationalism (16.25) and cosmopolitan orientation (23.84) reduce the perceived threat, yielding moderate support.
High ethnic nationalism and collective narcissism, combined with authoritarian support for order, strongly favor transparency mandates against foreign manipulation.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Extreme anti-elitism, low trust in government, and high populist people-centrism produce very high distrust in electoral integrity.

High trust in government and institutions, combined with low system justification and low dangerous worldview, produce mild opposition to distrusting elections, though very high neuroticism and fearfulness add some ambivalence.

Very high trust in government (69.6%), parliament (80.3%), and police (92.4%), combined with high system justification (69%) and authoritarian submission (92%), produce maximal opposition to distrusting elections, as this would undermine the legitimate authority and social order she strongly supports.

Very high system justification (88.19), trust in government (88.17), and very low populist attitudes (30.65) combined with low competitive jungle worldview produce strong trust in elections, i.e., low distrust.

Very high trust in government and institutions, combined with high authoritarianism, produce strong opposition to distrusting elections, though moderate populist attitudes slightly reduce the magnitude.
Very low political trust and system justification, combined with a dangerous worldview, produce strong distrust in elections, though high dutifulness prevents maximal opposition.

High system justification (80.2) and moderate trust in government (49.82) combined with high dutifulness (91.68) produce clear opposition to distrusting elections, though low trust in judiciary (22.12) and past justice system contact add slight nuance.
Extremely high system justification and trust in government, combined with low populist attitudes, produce strong opposition to distrusting elections.
